content:
How I Reorganized 45 Kubernetes Manifests (And You Should Too)

TL;DR: I took a messy flat structure of 45 manifest files and reorganized them into 8 logical component groups with clear dependencies, reducing confusion and deployment time. Here's how I did it and why it matters.

The Problem: 45 Files in One Directory

Imagine this:

talos/manifests/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ argocd-applications.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ argocd.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ cert_manager.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ cilium-bgp-config.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ cilium-ingressclass.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ cilium-ingress-lb.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ cilium-ingress-rbac.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ cilium-l2-ippool.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ cilium-loadbalancer-ippool.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ cilium-minimal.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ cilium-network-policies-examples.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ cilium-values-minimal.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ cilium-values.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ cilium.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ dns_admin.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ gateway-api-crds.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ gateway-api-examples.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ grafana-hubble-dashboard-configmap.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ headlamp-token.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ headlamp.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ ingress.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ jenkins.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ loki.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ longhorn-grafana-dashboard.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ longhorn-namespace.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ longhorn-recurring-jobs.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ longhorn-storage-classes.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ longhorn-values-minimal.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ longhorn-values.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ longhorn.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ metallb.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ minio.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ namespaces.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ openebs.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ portainer-ingress.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ portainer-traefik-ingress.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ portainer.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ prometheus-grafana.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ rook-ceph-cluster-values.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ rook-ceph-cluster.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ rook-ceph-operator-values.yaml
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ rook-ceph-operator.yaml
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ traefik-ingressroutes.yaml

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Real talk:

‚Ä¢ Finding related files? Good luck.

‚Ä¢ Understanding dependencies? Unclear.

‚Ä¢ Enabling/disabling components? Confusing.

‚Ä¢ New team member onboarding? Nightmare.

The Solution: Organized Component Groups

Here's what I built:

talos/manifests/
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ 00-namespaces/ ‚Üê Deploy 1st
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ 10-networking/ ‚Üê Cilium, Traefik
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ 20-security/ ‚Üê cert-manager, policies
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ 30-storage/ ‚Üê Longhorn, MinIO, OpenEBS
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ 40-observability/ ‚Üê Prometheus, Grafana, Loki
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ 50-management/ ‚Üê Portainer, Headlamp
‚îú‚îÄ‚îÄ 60-gitops/ ‚Üê ArgoCD, Jenkins
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ 70-loadbalancing/ ‚Üê MetalLB

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Benefits immediately visible:

‚úÖ Related files grouped together

‚úÖ Deployment order clear (00 ‚Üí 70)

‚úÖ Easy to find what you need

‚úÖ Component dependencies obvious

The Impact: Before vs After

BEFORE

üí• Pain Points:
‚Ä¢ 45 files in one directory
‚Ä¢ No clear structure
‚Ä¢ Hard to find components
‚Ä¢ Unclear dependencies
‚Ä¢ Confusing Terraform config
‚Ä¢ New team members lost
‚Ä¢ "Where's the Longhorn config?"

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

AFTER

‚ú® Improvements:
‚úÖ 8 organized component groups
‚úÖ Clear naming scheme (00-* through 70-*)
‚úÖ Dependency order built-in
‚úÖ Component READMEs with guides
‚úÖ Easy to enable/disable
‚úÖ New team members productive in minutes
‚úÖ "Longhorn? Check 30-storage/longhorn/"

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Deployment Order & Dependencies

START
‚Üì
[00-namespaces] ‚Üê Creates namespaces
‚Üì
[10-networking] ‚Üê Cilium, Traefik
‚Üì
[20-security] ‚Üê cert-manager, policies
‚Üì
[30-storage] ‚Üê Longhorn, MinIO, OpenEBS
‚Üì
[40-observability] ‚Üê Prometheus, Grafana, Loki
‚Üì
[50-management] ‚Üê Portainer, Headlamp
‚Üì
[60-gitops] ‚Üê ArgoCD, Jenkins
‚Üì
[70-loadbalancing] ‚Üê MetalLB
‚Üì
END

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Smart Part: Terraform automatically respects this order because of the numeric prefixes!

Real-World Example: Finding & Enabling Longhorn

BEFORE (Confusing)

$ grep -r "longhorn" .
# 15 results scattered everywhere
# Which one is the main one?
# Are there dependencies?
# Where's the config?

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

AFTER (Clear)

$ ls talos/manifests/30-storage/longhorn/
longhorn.yaml
longhorn-namespace.yaml
longhorn-values.yaml
longhorn-storage-classes.yaml
longhorn-recurring-jobs.yaml
longhorn-grafana-dashboard.yaml
README.md

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

To enable Longhorn:

# Edit talos/main.tf
longhorn = "manifests/30-storage/longhorn/longhorn.yaml"
# Deploy
tofu apply

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Crystal clear!

The Numbers

What I reorganized:

Metric
Value

Files Reorganized
45 manifests

Component Groups
8 organized groups

Documentation Created
21 files

Lines of Docs
10,000+

Components Documented
14

Environments Configured
4 (local/dev/staging/prod)

Time Saved (Long-term)
Hours per month

Key Learnings

1. Naming Matters

Numeric prefixes (00-, 10-, etc.) enforce deployment order naturally.

2. Documentation is Everything

Each component group has a README explaining what's included, when to use it, how to enable/disable, and common issues.

3. Dependencies Should Be Obvious

By looking at the folder structure, you know what depends on what, what can run independently, and what order to deploy in.

4. Multi-Environment is Essential

Having local/dev/staging/prod configs lets teams test safely, promote changes gradually, and learn without risk.

5. Enable/Disable Should Be Simple

One line in Terraform = deploy or remove a component

# To enable: uncomment
longhorn = "manifests/30-storage/longhorn/longhorn.yaml"
# To disable: comment
# longhorn = "manifests/30-storage/longhorn/longhorn.yaml"

Enter fullscreen mode

Exit fullscreen mode

Try It Yourself

My complete reorganized GitOps repo is open source:

‚≠ê github.com/beltagyy/gitops

You'll find:

‚Ä¢ Completely organized manifests

‚Ä¢ Multi-environment setup

‚Ä¢ 10,000+ lines of clear documentation

‚Ä¢ Component status matrix

‚Ä¢ Quick start guides

‚Ä¢ Real examples

What's Next?

I'm working on Phase 2:

‚Ä¢
App Deployment Scaffold - Deploy new apps in 30 seconds

‚Ä¢
Makefile Automation - Common commands via make

‚Ä¢
Local Dev Environment - 5-minute Kind cluster setup

‚Ä¢
Troubleshooting Runbooks - Self-service operations guide

Check out the GitHub Issues to see what's coming!

The Bottom Line

Organization matters. Especially at scale.

Taking time to organize your infrastructure code:

‚Ä¢ Saves hours long-term

‚Ä¢ Makes team onboarding faster

‚Ä¢ Reduces confusion & errors

‚Ä¢ Makes GitOps actually work

‚Ä¢ Improves code quality

If you're managing Kubernetes manifests, take a weekend to reorganize. Your future self will thank you. üöÄ
